title: Arrest Without Warrant in Bangladesh â€“ What Are Your Rights?
metaTitle: Arrested Without a Warrant in Bangladesh? What You Must Do Right Now (2026)
metaDescription: Arrested without a warrant in Bangladesh? Know your rights — what police can and cannot do, how long they can hold you, and when to call a lawyer. Act immediately.
heroIntro: A knock at the door. Officers in uniform. No warrant shown. Thousands of people in Bangladesh face arrest every year without any warrant being presented. Many do not know they have rights â€” rights that can be enforced if you act quickly and correctly.
toc: Can Police Arrest Without a Warrant in Bangladesh?

Your Rights During Arrest in Bangladesh

sections.h2: Can Police Arrest Without a Warrant in Bangladesh?
sections.content:
Yes â€” but only in specific circumstances defined by law. In Bangladesh, the power of police to arrest without a warrant is governed by the Code of Criminal Procedure 1898 (CrPC), primarily Section 54. Police cannot arrest simply on suspicion or personal discretion; there must be a legally recognisable ground.

A warrant is a written court order directing police to arrest a named person. When there is no warrant, police must be able to point to one of the situations listed under Section 54 to justify the arrest. If they cannot, the arrest is unlawful â€” and you have remedies. Contact a criminal lawyer in Dhaka or Uttara immediately if you believe an arrest was unlawful.

sections.h2: Legal Grounds for Warrantless Arrest (Section 54 CrPC)
sections.content:
Section 54 of the CrPC permits police to arrest without a warrant in the following situations:

  • Cognisable offence: A person suspected of committing or about to commit a cognisable offence (serious crimes like murder, robbery, or rape where police can act without a magistrate's order).
  • Known offender: A person who is a proclaimed offender under any law.
  • Possession of stolen property: A person found in possession of stolen goods or implements of housebreaking.
  • Arrest by another officer: A person for whom another police officer has a warrant.
  • Escape: A person who has escaped from lawful custody.
  • Concern in a cognisable case: A person concerned in or against whom a reasonable complaint is made.

It is important to note that Section 54 has been a subject of significant court scrutiny in Bangladesh. The High Court Division has repeatedly held that police cannot use Section 54 as a blanket power to arrest anyone at will.

sections.h2: Your Rights During Arrest in Bangladesh
sections.content:
The Bangladesh Constitution and CrPC provide explicit rights to every arrested person:

  • Article 33(1) of the Constitution: Every arrested person must be informed as soon as possible of the grounds of their arrest.
  • Right to a lawyer: You have the right to consult and be defended by a legal practitioner of your choice.
  • Produced before a Magistrate (Section 61 CrPC): Police must produce an arrested person before a Magistrate within 24 hours of arrest (excluding travel time). This is a constitutional right under Article 33(2).
  • No torture: The Constitution prohibits torture and cruel treatment. Any police officer who physically mistreats an arrested person commits a criminal offence.

These rights exist whether or not a warrant was issued. Knowing them and asserting them firmly â€” without being aggressive â€” can protect you significantly.

sections.h2: The Right to Know the Grounds of Arrest
sections.content:
One of the most important protections is the right to be told why you are being arrested. Under Article 33(1) of the Bangladesh Constitution, an arrested person must be told the grounds of their arrest "as soon as may be." Police cannot simply say "come with us" without providing a reason.

Practically, when arrested:

  1. Calmly ask: "On what grounds am I being arrested?" and "Is there a warrant or an FIR?"
  2. Do not physically resist â€” this can lead to additional charges.
  3. Note the names and badge numbers of arresting officers if possible.
  4. Immediately inform a family member or trusted person.
  5. Ask them to contact a bail lawyer in Dhaka urgently.

Once in custody, your rights continue:

  • Right to see a lawyer privately â€” police cannot deny this right, though in practice pressure may be applied. Insist firmly.
  • Right to medical examination â€” if you allege you have been tortured or are unwell. Demand this before being lodged in a cell.
  • Right to inform relatives â€” police are required to inform a family member or nominated person of your arrest and place of custody.
  • Remand (Section 167 CrPC): If the investigation cannot be completed within 24 hours, police must produce you before a Magistrate who may remand you to custody for further investigation. Remand orders can be challenged â€” a lawyer can file an application opposing remand.

sections.h2: How to Challenge an Unlawful Arrest
sections.content:
If you believe your arrest had no legal basis, there are several avenues:

  • Object at the time of arrest: Clearly state that the arrest is unlawful and that you are not committing any offence.
  • Apply for bail: Even for a warrantless arrest, an accused must be produced before a Magistrate who can grant bail if the offence is bailable â€” or the accused can apply for bail on non-bailable charges.
  • File a complaint against the officer: A false arrest can result in a complaint to the Superintendent of Police, the Police Commission, or a criminal complaint under Section 220 of the Penal Code against the officer.
  • Writ petition before the High Court Division: For a fundamentally unlawful arrest or detention, a writ of habeas corpus or a writ petition under Article 102 of the Constitution can bring immediate judicial scrutiny.

sections.h2: Habeas Corpus: Securing Release from Illegal Detention
sections.content:
If a person is detained unlawfully and bail is unavailable or denied, a writ of habeas corpus filed before the High Court Division of the Bangladesh Supreme Court is the most powerful remedy. The writ literally demands the authority to "produce the body" â€” i.e., to justify the detention before the court.

The High Court Division can:

  • Order the immediate release of the detained person
  • Examine whether the detention is lawful
  • Order production of the detainee in court
  • Initiate contempt proceedings if the order is not complied with

Habeas corpus is an emergency remedy and courts treat it with urgency. An experienced Supreme Court lawyer in Bangladesh should be contacted without delay if someone is unlawfully held.

sections.h2: Real-Life Scenario: Wrongful Arrest in a Family Dispute
sections.content:
Consider this common situation: A father and adult son have a property dispute. The son's wife files a false FIR alleging that the father threatened her. Based on this complaint, police arrive at the father's home one evening and take him to the station without showing any warrant or arrest order.

What should happen next:

  1. The father's family immediately contacts a bail lawyer in Dhaka.
  2. The lawyer verifies whether an FIR was actually filed and what offence is alleged.
  3. If the offence is non-bailable, an anticipatory bail application is filed urgently or, post-arrest, a regular bail application is filed before the Magistrate.
  4. If the arrest was made without any FIR or legal basis, a habeas corpus petition is filed in the High Court Division.
  5. A counter GD or complaint is filed documenting the false complaint.

This is exactly the kind of situation where having a lawyer on speed-dial makes the difference between spending a weekend in a police cell and being home the same evening.

faqs.question: Can police in Bangladesh arrest anyone without a warrant?
faqs.answer: No. Police can only arrest without a warrant in specific situations defined under Section 54 of the CrPC â€” such as when a cognisable offence has been committed or is about to be committed.

faqs.question: What should I do if police arrest me without a warrant?
faqs.answer: Remain calm, ask for the grounds of arrest, do not physically resist, inform a family member immediately, and contact a criminal lawyer as soon as possible.

faqs.question: Must police produce me before a Magistrate within 24 hours?
faqs.answer: Yes. This is a constitutional right under Article 33(2). Police must produce an arrested person before the nearest Magistrate within 24 hours of arrest (excluding travel time).

faqs.question: Can I sue police for unlawful arrest?
faqs.answer: Yes. You can file a criminal complaint or a civil suit for wrongful confinement. A writ petition before the High Court Division can also address unlawful police action.

faqs.question: What is Section 54 of the CrPC?
faqs.answer: Section 54 of the CrPC is the primary provision allowing police to arrest without a warrant in Bangladesh. It lists specific grounds and is not a blanket power.
